sentences.text: That's right.
I just like add, we arrived in a big military plane, a Hermes, I understand.
And we-- when we came down, we were greeted with a carnation, you know, and a whole lot of people who greeted us.
And they said, it's, you know, it's-- it's nice to see you.
How did you manage even to look like that?
And still have-- and even to smile?
We were pleased to be back, you know.
Anyway, the first thing they did, when we arrived, they took us to the Croix-Rousse, the hospital that was in Lyon.
And that love-- that food that was put in front of us, I remember, all of us, we took one spoonful of potatoes, and we couldn't eat anymore.
You see, our stomach had shrunk to nothing.
So we were crying over the food.
You know, we were looking at the food, and crying over the food, because we saw bread, and we saw potatoes, and we saw meat, and we saw wine.
And we couldn't eat it.
And we were taken up.
And the first thing they did, they gave us an injection to us, that cocktail of injection they gave us.
The needle looked very, very big, you know?
And especially when you had no  more flesh, except skin and bones.
I weighed exactly 37 kilos, so it wasn't very big.
We were all very light, light weights.
And he put that needle in.
And it seemed an awful long needle to put into a skin, into my arm.
The next thing they did, they did it already in Theresienstadt.
They had special lamps to see the lice that were in your skin.
We were full of not ordinary lice, skin lice.
They eat up, they make trenches in your arm.
You know, they eat you up.
And they could see them on your tummy and everywhere.
So the next thing they did, they put us in a cocktail of chemicals, and bathed us in there.
We had no hair, so they probably just washed the hair.
A little bit of hair we had.
And we were put into quarantine.
Each of us got home.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we were under medical supervision.
It was very little food, a little at the time.
But we were home now, and we wanted to know who came home, and who is alive.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So my Janine, her parents, and her sister were deported.
But thank God, the rest of the family survived, you know, uncle and aunts, and so on, so forth.
They heard, in Saint-Etienne, that a convoy of French girls are-- came back from Theresienstadt, and are, at the moment, at the Croix-Rousse in Lyon.
So being from Alsace, and being good bakers, they made a beautiful cherry tart.
It was cherry time.
And they came.
And we asked them, have you heard if any of my family had come back.
And she said, I think there is one brother of yours that is in Saint-Etienne.
So she was trying to get in touch with him.
The next thing that she did, we were not supposed to go down.
You were in quarantine.
So we found a way of one getting down, and getting that famous tart-- pie.
And we bought it up.
We put it on the bed.
The two of us in one bed, and we cut it right in the middle, you know, each should have half.
So I started to eat, and I could see that I wouldn't be able to eat.
And she had a-- she really fancied that tart.
The thing that she didn't realize was that, every cherry that went into her stomach showed up.
And she had to have her stomach washed out.
Because she couldn't take it.
You see, we were not-- we couldn't take food.
So that was the story of the pie, that lovely pie.
But it didn't do us any good.
But we liked that pie.
So the aunt got herself in touch with my brother Armand.
He came.
He couldn't see me.
He could only see me from the window in a hospital room, and he was in the courtyard.
And he looked fat, but very bad.
And he told us that he was liberated in Auschwitz, when, by a sheer chance, that he survived.
Because, apparently, they were all rounded up [NON-ENGLISH], in the middle, and they were going to all be shot at the last minute.
Somehow, they didn't-- they didn't do it.
And he was liberated.
And, of course, when the camp was liberated, they, some of the inmates, or the people in the camp, knew exactly where the food was that the Red Cross sent and nobody got.
And they went up and raided it, that reserve there.
But what they didn't know was that it was very bad for you to drink condensed milk and rich food.
And they made themselves ill, you see?
So when I saw him, he was all puffed up around, grey and green, the colorings, you see, very bad colorings.
But he had been-- he came home through Odessa.
They were sent from Auschwitz to Odessa, and from Odessa they came home to Marseilles.
So he was the first one to come home to Saint-Etienne.
And we had made-- had said, if any of us survived, we should come back to Saint-Etienne from where we were the last address.
Not to go to Rouen, you know, there.
So he was the first one to come back, and didn't find anybody.
Eventually, he tried to find out if my brother David survived.
And he found out that he was liberated by the Americans.
And he was in Paris, very ill, and in the good hands of the American at L'Hotel Lutetia, which was turned into a hospital.
So he was getting better, also, but fed spoonful at the time.
Eventually, we stayed there.
We were quite a few-- a plane full I suppose-- not airplane, it wasn't planes like today.
It was a small plane.
It must have been 30 to 40 girls coming home, some in stretchers, you know, in hammocks.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] now, everybody was searching, look-- asking questions.
Even at that stage, knowing what we had-- and knowing and having seen what we saw, we still lived and hoped.
We didn't give up hope, against all odds.
So we-- we-- we met up.
My brother thought I looked terrible, you know, because no hair, very thin, and so on, but I was just about alive.
So, after a fortnight in quarantine, something had to be done.
Here we were, alone, with nothing, not even a pair of-- no clothing to put on our backs, nothing.
What are we going to do?
So the American joint at the time, I think, came in.
And we were provided with some kind of clothing.

And we were taken for convalescence to Chambon-sur-Lignon, which, at the time when I was taken, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and still there's no-- and still ignorant that 60 kilometers away from Saint-Etienne, 5,000 Jews were hidden by the Huguenots in Chambon-sur-Lignon.
I didn't know it.
I didn't know it after the war.
Only the last few years, I know it. 5,000 Jewish people were saved there.
No.
No.
Because the people I knew didn't know.
Because they would have been there, you know?
The one in Saint-Etienne.
They didn't know either.
So for three months, we were convalescing.
And the doctors said that, for four years, we shouldn't marry, or have children.
Why was that?
Because you were not in a physical state.
And we had-- we had a monitor, who took us for walks, and hikes.
And we were taught how to dance, and how to-- get into a bit of life again into us.
And then, after three months, we were still nowhere.
Yes.
We are now with my two brothers, and Janine.
The others came from other cities, you know, some of the other girls, so they went to the other cities.
But we remained in Saint-Etienne.
And she was taken in Raguhn, so she stayed with us already, you see?
She liked my brother, so she-- she remained with us.
She had a large family, but she still remained with us, then, in Le Chambon.
After that, she went to her family in Strasbourg.
That family, judges, and doctors, and so on, and very religious, didn't suit her at all, that very religious.
She had thrown the almighty over.
She didn't want to have anything to do any more with religion.
She was against God.
There was no God.
And that's it.
And with me, I come in here, I come in very late with it, I felt, from the very beginning, that death was on our doorstep.
It was there.
And I said, really and truly, were we all meant to die.
And I kept believing, because I needed something more than just fight for my life.
I needed to believe that it can't be that everybody will die.
I needed something to hold on.
So I was-- I remained a kind of believer, a believer in God.
Perhaps not in everything else, a believer in God.
And I think, in my case, it helped me.
In her case, it was the opposite.
That what she saw, she didn't-- there is no God.
If this can happen, there is no God.
Therefore, even to this day, she's a little bit more in need to go to a synagogue or something like this, but I don't know how much she still believes.
Because she lost, after all, her parents and her sister.
She died in Auschwitz on the night, in April, 1945.
She had tuberculosis, you didn't survive.
My brother, Armand, saw her before she died.
She didn't make it.
You see, she was separated.
And, afterwards, she was all alone.
We were with other French girl, Suzann, that was all the time with us, and so on.
We have to keep a certain-- we were always little groups together.
You had to keep in little groups.
If you were alone, then you went down very fast.
Some women were-- some were with mothers and daughters.
And unfortunately, I saw some of the very great Jewish families in France-- bankers, big stores holders, who were also finished off in Auschwitz, and did not survive.
There was one woman particular, I think her name was Lazarus, a mother and daughter, terribly nice lady with her daughter.
And I saw her sometimes searching in rubbish to find something for her daughter.
She made it as far as Bergen-Belsen.
She was still alive.
And on that other journey from Bergen-Belsen to Raguhn, she died, and then the daughter died.
Yeah, they didn't make it.
But this-- this were wonderful people, you know.
And they, you would have thought, had all the means to escape, or to fight, or to find a little hiding place.
In the end, it was a matter of luck not to be caught.
So I'm coming back to France.
So after they gave us this little rest in-- up in the mountains, they gave us some kind of money to find an apartment.
And we were given a bed, a table, and little stools, a little bit of crockery.
This is from the joint--
Joint American.
America.
And that, and now they-- and a little bit of money, you know, just to cover you for months or so.
And then you had to find a way of looking after yourself.
Now, the thing is, my brother was offered, the oldest one, a place at university for nothing.
Everything free.
The second one was offered a place at an academy of couture, French couture house [NON-ENGLISH].
So he went there.
And the little brother wasn't here any more.
So the older one wanted to do that here.
But Janine didn't want any more to remain in Strasbourg, but wanted to marry my brother.
See?
He gave up his studies and got married.
He couldn't  have done both?
She was prepared to do anything.
But she said she can't accept do both.
I think, to this day, he has some kind of regrets.
Because he was, number one, a bright scholar.
He would have done very well.
But he did all right.
But he still regrets that he had his studies interrupted.
So what did he do?
What did we do?
We went back to Sarreguemines.
No, first of all, we got in touch with this lady from Blamont.
And this lady, who, during the war, when we were so hard up with money and everything, we asked her to send us something.
She said, please don't write.
Otherwise, they'll find out that I'm holding Jewish goods.
So we didn't.
So, but, after the war, when we went back to Blamont, that good lady had kept everything for us.
Therefore, very bad business people we were at the time, we managed to sell that all, probably much too cheap.
But we got the first few francs together like that.
Then we went on to Sarreguemines, where, of course, once we were not there, the others had taken everything-- the furniture, the carpets, the lot.
We recovered some of it.
So we got some of the furniture back, some of the rugs back, and some linen back, and things like that, that helped us to furnish the flat.
See, we only got a bed, and a little table, little table.
So that furnished a little bit of the apartment, and a bit of the crockery we got back.
Terrible, because, of course, they're-- none of them collaborated, you know?
None of them did anything, you know.
And-- well, ahead of the war, very brave people who were in the resistance, you know, were sent out away from Alsace-Lorraine because they were not-- they were not for the Germans.
So, yes, we had that, too.
But they were willing to give-- we didn't get everything back, of course, but we got something back.
People knew who it belonged to, and they gave it to us back.
And, so we had something to start.
Then we had-- we made a little bit of money by selling the jewelry, watches, and things like that.
And we wanted to start in business.
So we-- we got in touch with a company, and they give us goods.
So, the two of us, my brother and I, we started in business.
We didn't do so badly.
Then we started to think that we could do-- he you could do better if he went into a-- into a linen, you know, materials or whatever, manufacture.
And first into linen.
I got in touch with a company I worked for, whose daughter was deported, but it's a very small world.
Because a company I worked for in Blamont was a company that did percale and velvet and all that.
And-- and they were very nice directors.
My mother kept saying they paid me too well, you know.
And-- and their daughter was deported.
And what I didn't realize it till last-- till last year, was the son married Simone Weil.
Simone Weil was married to the son.
But, anyway, I went to see these people.
And you couldn't get merchandise in those days, you know, for love or money.
But these people started us off.
And that's how we started in there.
And now he is-- then, afterwards, he became a manufacturer of-- of clothing for the food manufacturers, you know, and hotels.
But that's how he started.
But, at first, by that family, where I worked, who gave him the first start, and he didn't finish his studies.
But he made sure that his sons-- and he has three sons and one daughter-- that [INAUDIBLE] they finished their studies.
So that's how we actually started.
And marriage, I went-- eventually, when he got married to Janine, I went to Paris.
I lived in Paris.
And-- for a while.
And I worked for a lady that had two sons who came from Bratislava, and was a very fine corsetiere.
And we started to manufacture underwear.
And we did very well.
And it was easy, really, after the war, to do relatively well, because there was nothing.
Because when I tried to sell these things in-- in-- in-- in the north of France, everything was flat.
This was completely bombarded.
The north of France was all-- There was-- only ruins.
The first shops were shacks, you know, where they sold the goods.
They were only too pleased to see goods.
Anyway, in 19-- we found out, through an aunt of ours, that we had relations in England.
Some, not direct, you know, second cousins.
And we got in touch with them.
And so I came on holiday to England one year.
And then I came another year, in 1949, on holiday.
And that's when I met my husband.
I married very fast.
I met him and he asked me to marry.
They were a little bit related.
He asked me if I would marry him three weeks later.
I was engaged six weeks later.
And I was married three months later, so it was a--
He was.
He was living in England.
He worked-- they lived in England.
English.
They were English nationality.
And I came over.
When I got married, I came over here.
That's how I came to England.
Unfortunately, he-- he was taken ill two and a half years later, and he died.
Even before, we were married three years.
I had, by then, Sandra, who was 21 months, and the younger ones was six weeks old when he died.
Nothing.
No husband.
Very hard.
It was a very-- it was a time when I said to myself, why did I survive.
To go through that again?
Now, understand, I was at a complete loss this time.
It was like a mountain had fallen, gone down under the sea.
It was-- it was a terrible loss, because I lost already my parents.
I lost-- I saw the murder of so many people, the dying of so many people.
And then I found a little bit of happiness, and that leaves you.
I could have really gone the completely other way, you know, of religion.
But my husband was a religious man.
And he didn't 100% trust my religion, you know?
My kind of religion, because he didn't think I was so profoundly religious.
I said I believed in God.
But all the little things that some people make a big fuss were not that important to me.
Because a good human being was important.
These little things, that you-- the laws and so on.
The ritual of--
Too much of it wasn't that important.
But, anyway, I felt terrible, because he was a marvelous father.
And he would have been a marvelous father.
And he was just getting married, and he had a family, and so on, and so forth.
And yet-- and he didn't want to die.
And yet, and he died.
So I felt bad for him, that he had died so young.
And I felt bad because the children missed a wonderful father.
So-- and, then, of course, then everything changed.
But I was terribly unhappy.
I was very, very depressed.
And I must say, I had hardly any support.
No support.
Because you didn't know people?
I did know very few.
I live in the country.
The few people I knew were very good, once I made friends with.
But it's amazing, in circumstances like that, how his old friends were not supportive at all.
And my English wasn't that good, either.
I can't say that after two and a half years it was that good.
I wasn't used to dealing in-- in-- in anything concerning bills or anything, banking or whatever.
All that was strange still to me.
And although I had my family, you know, the cousins, but I was still on my own, terribly on my own.
I considered, one time, to go to France, but I wanted to bring them up religiously, because that's what my husband wanted for his daughters.
And I had also some-- some rights, which I had to see to, which didn't permit me just to leave everything and go.
So that it took me, really, another 20 years till I got part of my rights.
I had a 20 year fight.
This was financial things.
Financial fight, yeah.
By that time, I had already brought up my children, by that time.
I had a very, very hard time.
Oh, it was just financially very difficult.
It was a lonely life.
It was also a life where I was scared to marry again.
I now realize that there were many factors why I didn't marry again.
Because I had the impression, at the time, whatever I touch, I lose.
Do you understand?
You get that feeling that you love something, or you love somebody, and it goes.
You lose it.
It leaves you with a trauma.
And you mustn't forget that I still had terrible nightmares.
I was still not that well.
And I have had two little one-- children, that needed me badly.
But they have difficulties, because they know what I went through.
I find it just, still, to this day, difficult for them to read about it, or watch anything about it, because they know this their mother went through that.
And they can't cope with it.
And why I do it, really, is for the next generation, and for the other, for later, that people shouldn't forget.
Because the last thing we must do is forget.
Because we do it.
We don't do it for ourselves.
We feel a guilt complex.
There's a terrible thing that I will never forget.
If we don't do it, we feel guilty.
If we do it, we feel bad.
You feel ill.
And yet, we feel we have to do it.
Because, in the memory of the ones that didn't come back, we feel, all the time, that we have more than a duty.
It's something-- we are here because we must tell the world.
And I think that it is something this is forever with us.
And what people don't understand, is that when we watch something, we always see something else.
The others' flesh is constantly with us.
And yet, you have to try and make a life for yourself.
